data.title: Umicore targets €720M to €780M adjusted EBITDA for 2025 amid efficiency measures and CapEx reductions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Umicore SA (OTCPK:UMICF) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Bart Sap highlighted full-year revenues of €3.5 billion, an adjusted EBITDA of €763 million, and an EBITDA margin of 22%. Operational cash flow remained strong at €384 million, while CapEx was reduced to €555 million, significantly below the mid-year target of €650 million.
  • Sap emphasized the robust performance of the foundation businesses, including a 26% EBITDA margin in Catalysis and a 36% EBITDA margin in Recycling, despite lower revenues in the Recycling segment. Specialty Materials underperformed with an EBITDA margin below 20%, attributed to challenges in the cobalt and specialty materials market.
  • Battery Materials continued to face headwinds due to a slowdown in electric vehicle demand. Umicore paused construction of its Canadian Battery Materials plant while reallocating customer contracts to its Korean facilities. Sap noted a €1.6 billion impairment in H1 2024 but reiterated the company’s focus on value recovery.
  • CFO Wannes Peferoen reported an 11% year-over-year decline in revenues, driven by the slowdown in electrification and global vehicle production. Adjusted net profit group share was €255 million, resulting in an adjusted EPS of €1.06. The net result group share stood at (-€1.48 billion) due to impairments.
  • Peferoen highlighted efficiency measures that generated €100 million in 2024, well ahead of the €70 million target, and outlined further cost-saving initiatives for 2025.

Outlook

  • Umicore projects adjusted EBITDA for 2025 to range between €720 million and €780 million. This guidance excludes potential impacts from geopolitical tensions or tariffs.
  • For Battery Materials, adjusted EBITDA is expected to remain similar to 2024, excluding one-off effects. Catalysis is forecasted to maintain its strong performance from prior years, while Recycling earnings are anticipated to decline as favorable PGM hedges roll off. Specialty Materials is expected to slightly improve.
  • The company plans to reduce CapEx by an additional 20% in 2025 and increase cumulative efficiency savings to €200 million, up from the initial target of €100 million.

Financial Results

  • Group revenues for 2024 were €3.5 billion, reflecting an 11% decrease year-over-year. Adjusted EBITDA stood at €763 million, down €209 million compared to the previous year.
  • Free operating cash flow remained strong at €384 million, supported by a €392 million reduction in net working capital and a 35% year-over-year reduction in CapEx.
  • Adjusted EBIT was €478 million, while the adjusted effective tax rate increased to 29% due to higher provisioning for uncertain tax positions. Net financial debt rose to €1.4 billion, with a leverage ratio of 1.87x.
  • Hedging strategies provided substantial support for rhodium and palladium prices, mitigating some impacts of a less favorable trading environment.

Q&A

  • Ranulf Orr, Citi: Asked about updates on the IONWAY timeline and minimum returns from equity injections. CFO Peferoen stated the project is on track with bank confirmation of its viability. He noted project financing is targeted for the second half of 2026.
  • Chetan Udeshi, JPMorgan: Raised concerns about CapEx spending in Battery Materials despite underutilized facilities. Sap attributed CapEx to debottlenecking in Korea and completing European projects to fulfill customer commitments. Peferoen outlined mitigation strategies for potential trade tariffs through Umicore’s diversified footprint and supply chains.
  • Geoff Haire, UBS: Inquired about exiting larger Battery Materials contracts to save CapEx. Sap emphasized maintaining strong customer commitments and highlighted ongoing efforts for portfolio diversification in Europe.
  • Tristan Lamotte, Deutsche Bank: Asked about restructuring costs, metal hedge roll-offs, and expected EBITDA from take-or-pay agreements. Peferoen clarified that €70 million in restructuring costs were provisioned in 2024, with cash-out expected in 2025. Sap noted take-or-pay provisions would provide EBITDA support but declined to guide for 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were concerned about the financial strain from ongoing CapEx in Battery Materials and the impact of rolling off metal hedges. They pressed management on cost-saving measures and the viability of long-term investments like IONWAY, reflecting a slightly negative tone.
  • Management maintained a defensive but measured tone, citing strong contractual commitments and efficiency gains. Sap emphasized a focus on value recovery in Battery Materials and highlighted operational successes in foundation businesses.
  • Compared to prior quarters, the tone was more cautious, reflecting the challenging market environment and the need to address structural headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • No comparison to the previous quarter was possible as the previous transcript was unavailable. However, current initiatives, including efficiency measures and CapEx reductions, indicate an intensified focus on cost discipline.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted heavily to Battery Materials and IONWAY, reflecting concerns about capital allocation and return timelines.
  • Management’s tone signaled a cautious but forward-looking approach, with an emphasis on mitigating risks and optimizing operations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Battery Materials remains a key challenge due to underutilized capacity and a slowdown in EV demand. Management paused Canadian plant construction but continues to invest in Korea and Europe to meet customer commitments.
  • Rolling off favorable metal hedges in Recycling poses risks to profitability in 2025 and 2026.
  • Restructuring costs and geopolitical uncertainties, including potential trade tariffs, could impact financial performance.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about CapEx in Battery Materials, with specific questions on long-term returns and free cash flow generation.

Final Takeaway

Umicore faced significant headwinds in 2024, including a €1.6 billion impairment in Battery Materials and a challenging EV market. Despite these challenges, the company delivered robust performance in Catalysis and Recycling while implementing €100 million in efficiency gains. The outlook for 2025 includes adjusted EBITDA guidance of €720 million to €780 million, supported by additional cost-saving measures and a 20% reduction in CapEx. However, risks related to Battery Materials performance, geopolitical uncertainties, and rolling off metal hedges remain key concerns for investors. The company’s strategic focus on efficiency and operational optimization will be critical going forward.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: American Axle & Manufacturing Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.06 beats by $0.10, revenue of $1.38B misses by $10M
data.description:
• American Axle & Manufacturing press release (NYSE:AXL): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of -$0.06 beats by $0.10.
  • Revenue of $1.38B (-5.5% Y/Y) misses by $10M.

  • AAM's full year 2025 financial targets are as follows:

    • AAM is targeting sales in the range of $5.8- $6.05 billion.
    • AAM is targeting Adjusted EBITDA in the range of $700 - $760 million.
    • AAM is targeting Adjusted free cash flow in the range of $200 - $230 million; this target assumes capital spending of approximately 5% of sales.

data.title: DaVita extends post-Q4 drop as Berkshire Hathaway cuts stake
data.description:
• DaVita (NYSE:DVA) continued to trade lower in the premarket on Friday as an insider sale from its major shareholder Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) worsened a selloff caused by the company’s 2025 outlook issued with its Q4 2024 results.
  • According to an SEC filing on Thursday, the Omaha, Nebraska-based conglomerate led by billionaire investor Warren Buffett has sold approximately 203.1K shares of dialysis services provider for $156.01 apiece.
  • With the Tuesday's transaction worth approximately $31.7M, Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A), an owner with 10% or more holdings of DaVita (NYSE:DVA), has cut its stake in the company by ~1%.
  • Denver, Colorado-based DVA was a notable decliner in the postmarket trading on Thursday after the company set its 2025 adjusted earnings outlook at $10.20-$11.30 per share, below $11.44 per share in the consensus.

data.title: Owens Corning to sell glass reinforcements business to Praana Group for $755 million
data.description:
• Owens Corning (NYSE:OC) and Praana Group on Friday said they have signed a definitive agreement for the sale of Owens Corning’s glass reinforcements business to Praana Group at $755 million.
  • The transaction is expected to close in 2025.
  • Owens Corning’s glass reinforcements business generated revenue of about $1.1 billion in 2024 and has approximately 4,000 employees across a global footprint that includes 18 operations in 12 countries.
  • OC +0.53% premarket to $179.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Intel-Taiwan Semiconductor likely 'won't work,' Citi says
data.description:
Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) shares have gained nearly 25% over the past five trading sessions, in part due to speculation that the semiconductor giant could enter into some sort of tie up with Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) for its foundry operations.

And while such a deal may be batted around in Washington, D.C. because of geopolitical concerns, investment firm Citi believes it “won't work” for three key reasons.

“First, Intel employees would have to subjugate themselves to TSMC employees and directions,” Citi analyst Christopher Danely wrote in a note to clients. “Second, Intel and TSMC have completely different manufacturing process ecosystems and methodologies so Intel employees would have to learn completely different ways of manufacturing.”

“Lastly, Intel workers would likely have to adopt completely foreign working arrangements that exist in Taiwan such as living near the factory, being able to come to the factory at a moment’s notice in the middle of the night if needed, etc,” Danely added.

Danely kept his Neutral rating on Intel and reiterated his stance that the company should exit the foundry business and keep its focus on its “core microprocessor competency.”

The Santa Clara, Calif.-based chip giant has struggled over the years to turn itself around and compete with Taiwan Semiconductor and other global foundries, as it looks to cash in on the boom for making processors for other companies.

In its most recent quarter, Intel Foundry generated $4.5B in revenue, largely from Intel's own design arm. The company announced in September that it would manufacture some chips for Amazon (AMZN) Web Services.

The majority of AI chips are made by Taiwan Semiconductor and, to a lesser extent, Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). Taiwan Semiconductor has a fab in Arizona and has spoken to Nvidia (NVDA) about making its Blackwell line of GPUs at the Arizona plant.

data.title: Fannie Mae posts Q4 earnings rise, revenue slips, delinquencies increase
data.description:
Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) stock slid 3.8% in Friday premarket trading after the government-sponsored enterprise posted a $321M provision for credit losses in Q4 as serious delinquency rates increased for both single-family and multifamily loans.

Q4 comprehensive income of $4.13B rose from $4.05B in Q3.

Q4 revenue of $7.30B declined from $7.34B in the prior quarter. Q4 net interest income of $7.18B fell 1% Q/Q.

Fanne Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) Q4 provision for credit losses of $321M vs. benefit of $27M in Q3.

Net investment losses of $10M reversed from net investment gain of $12M in the previous quarter.

Net worth climbed to $94.7B from $90.5B at Sept. 30, 2024.

"Our strong results were driven by guaranty fee income, consistent with the transformation of our business model that began well over a decade ago," said President and CEO Priscilla Almodovar. "For the year, we provided $381 billion in liquidity to the U.S. housing market, helping 1.4 million households buy, refinance, or rent a home."

Administrative expenses of $947M increased from $925M in the prior quarter.

During the year, Fannie Mae (OTCQB:FNMA) acquired ~778,000 single-family purchase loans, of which about half were first time homebuyers, and ~204,000 of single-family refinance loans.

In Q4, the U.S. weekly average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage rate rose to 6.85% at the end of 2024 from 6.08% in Q3.

The single-family serious delinquency rate increased to 0.56% from 0.52% in Q2 and 0.55% in Q4 2023. The multifamily serious delinquency rate of 0.57% ticked up from 0.56% in the prior quarter and 0.46% a year ago.

Conference call at 8:00 AM ET.

Earlier, Federal National Mortgage Association reports Q4 results
